statement: Iceland's compliance with transposing EEA-relevant EU legislation has been imperfect. As of 2025, the EFTA Surveillance Authority (ESA) reported an incorporation deficit of approximately 1.5% — meaning about 200 EEA-relevant directives had not been transposed into Icelandic law within the required deadline. ESA opened 25–35 formal infringement cases per year against Iceland in 2020–2024, comparable to medium-performing EU member states. The backlog has been a source of friction, with the EU criticising Iceland for delays in transposing financial services and data protection legislation.
source_name: EFTA Surveillance Authority — Internal Market Scoreboard 2024
caveats: Incorporation deficit metrics measure timeliness, not substantive compliance. Some delays reflect Iceland's small civil service capacity rather than political resistance. EU member states also have significant transposition deficits — Iceland's record is comparable to or better than several EU members.

related_entries.statement: Since the EEA Agreement entered into force in 1994, Iceland has incorporated approximately 13,000 EU legal acts into Icelandic law through the EEA Joint Committee. As of 2025, the EEA acquis covers roughly 75% of the EU single market legislation, excluding agriculture, fisheries, customs union, trade policy, justice and home affairs, foreign policy, and the euro. Iceland has a formal right to veto EEA-relevant legislation through the 'right of reservation' under Article 102 EEA, but this right has never been exercised due to the severe consequences — it would trigger suspension of the affected part of the EEA Agreement.

Under the EEA Agreement, EFTA states (Iceland, Norway, Liechtenstein) must adopt EU internal market legislation but have no voting rights in the EU Council or European Parliament. They participate in 'decision-shaping' through expert committees and consultation, but not 'decision-making'.

citations.report_title: „Við hefðum náð meiri árangri með því að vera innan Evrópusambandsins“

claims.claim_text: Utanríkisráðherra telur íslensk stjórnvöld ekki hafa komið nægilega snemma að ETS-ferlinu til að tryggja betri skilyrði.
